text: A eight-year-old man was admitted in emergency for a four-hour onset of acute abdominal pain. Previous medical history was consistent with six months non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs used for chronic abdominal pain. Physical examination disclosed acute peritoneitis with diffuse abdominal guarding and rebound turdiness. Hemodynamic status was normal. with slight tachycardia. Emergent CT scan showed three air bubbles next to a jejunal tract.

During laparoscopic exploration, the surgeon observes localized inframesocolic acute peritonitis with visible small bowel content and fibrin deposits. The exploration reveals a covert perforation in a solitary diverticulum located in the proximal jejunum.

The surgeon suspends the small bowel to the abdominal wall using percutaneous sutures. The jejunal tract is transected with an endo-GIA linear stapler, and the mesentery is divided using a ligature Maryland, two centimeters from the bowel wall. The two small bowel ends are positioned side-to-side, isoperistaltic, and suspended to the abdominal wall. A side-to-side jejuno-jejunal intracorporeal anastomosis is performed using the endo-GIA linear stapler through two small enterotomies, which are then closed with a barbed running suture.

The surgeon applies a first extra-mucosal layer followed by a second seromuscular layer of sutures. A reinforcing non-absorbable monofilament suture is placed at the lower edge, and the mesenteric defect is closed. The abdominal cavity is thoroughly lavaged with two liters of saline, and a 24 French silicone drain is positioned. The resected jejunal tract is extracted into an endo bag for inspection.
Post-operative course was uneventful and the patient was discharged at home on the sixth post-operative day.
